narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to flag concerns based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your Year 1 school decile rank of 4—below the typical student's 5.71—and especially your Year 3 rank of 1, which is much lower than the average 5.53. Your LSAT score of 26 also stands out as notably below the dataset average of 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 is under the usual 3.21. As a female Asian student from a lower-middle income family planning to attend part-time, you're in somewhat underrepresented groups—women make up 43.6% of applicants, Asians 5.9%, and part-timers just 7.3%—but these played smaller roles overall.

The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which heavily pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure since it's so far below what's typical for successful students. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which similarly nudged the outlook toward failure compared to higher-scoring peers. Your Year 1 decile rank came third, again contributing to the risk because it lagged behind averages. Family income bracket ranked fourth in sway, tilting toward higher failure odds in line with patterns for lower-middle incomes.

Your undergraduate GPA followed as the fifth biggest factor, reinforcing the concern with its below-average mark, while your racial background as Asian was sixth and also leaned toward predicting struggle. Planning part-time study was seventh, a bit riskier than the full-time norm of 92.7% of students. Interestingly, your gender as female was the least influential and actually slightly favored a passing prediction, bucking the trend just a touch.

Overall, your profile shows weaker academic markers than most students who pass the bar, putting you at elevated risk. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and build a stronger final-year record to lift those decile ranks. With targeted prep like bar-focused tutoring, even part-time students from similar backgrounds have turned predictions around—keep pushing, and reapply once you've shored up these areas.
